body_txt: If a game designer wants an NPC to be invulnerable, they'd better make sure that they truly are. The Lord British Postulate was coined in this WoW Insider post, and is the cornerstone of the creativity and ingenuity of the more cunning player. In short, if a Non-Player Character cannot be killed through normal means, then there must be some abnormal means to do the job. Quite often, if a character's weapons fail to do the duty, then the task falls upon other NPCs , world objects, or the environment to assassinate the relevant target with some prodding from the player characters. The Lord British Postulate is not restricted to MMORPGs , but can be applied to C RPGs , FPSes , even Tabletop RPGs . The name references the fact that there is a way to murder Lord British in nearly every Ultima game, famously including Ultima Online , where Lord British was killed by a fire spell because his invulnerability flags hadn't been set properly after a server crash. That said, the Lord British Postulate has less grounding in reality than one would assume. A great number of NPCs are free to walk around unmolested, safe in their invulnerability. However, the more important the NPC is, the more likely it will be the target of a creative assassination attempt. A king is a bigger target than the common baker and much more satisfying to dispatch. This is most common in Western RPGs , as they are usually programmed with "player freedom" in mind, which means all characters, from the Player Character , to the bandits you are clearly meant to kill , to the villagers you can kill ( but shouldn't ), all the way to the king that the game does everything to prevent you from killing (making him the target of this trope) are all treated as the same "Entity" class by the game, which gives them all Hit Points , an inventory, a death state, and the ability to be affected by combat mechanics. In other genres, enemies and NPCs tend to be considered completely different types of objects, so the concept of death ( at least due to game mechanics ) does not apply at all to any character who wasn't specifically programmed as an enemy. For such games, this trope usually requires something like a Hopeless Boss Fight , Cutscene Boss , or Puzzle Boss . In a variant, the character is not literally killed, but neutralized, in a way that wouldn't be normally possible and might disrupt a story. For example, if you can manage to make an important character fall into a pit from where they cannot get out, while being supposed to be around. This trope can be an applied form of Loophole Abuse with how you go about killing Lord British (or any other normally unkillable NPC ). Does not apply to a character who is not clearly intended to be unkillable. Just because no reward is offered for killing a character does not mean the developers intended them to be unkillable. This only applies when they are invulnerable to all normal forms of attack, or the developers have stated they are intended to be invulnerable. Other hints that a character might be intended not to be killable are very quick escapes before you could reasonably set up an attack, or implausible (compared to other equivalent things) stats that would make a confrontation normally Unwinnable by Design . Having a lot of hit points per se is not evidence, but having unrealistically much more hit points than any other challenge in the game, to the point that killing is extremely implausible and slow to achieve, is evidence. It also does not apply to techniques used to solo kill bosses that are intended to be killed by larger groups, or to defeat something that would be difficult (but not impossible or implausible) without a special item. For example, killing an ice giant which is very resistant to any other form of damage without fire isn't this trope, even if the game presents you with an optional quest to retrieve a fire sword exactly for this, unless by doing so some scripts break and the game derails. But killing an ice giant which is coded to be damaged only by fire without using it is definitely this trope. If the game acknowledges a death, untimely and unexpected it might be, the trope is possibly not at play, although in certain cases it might be a joke on the part of the developers. Savvy developers will code in what happens if you do manage to kill them. Compare Murphy's Law , a similar caution regarding the misuse of technology, and Defeating the Undefeatable , the story/plot counterpart of this trope. Contrast with Invulnerable Civilians , which simply can't be killed, if they can even be engaged in combat at all. A Hopeless Boss Fight or Invincible Minor Minion is seen as a challenge to these people. Can lead to the game being Unintentionally Unwinnable if the loss of the NPC screws you over. This has nothing to do with the other ship from Gradius . Warning: do not add examples that are just very difficult to execute, but doable . If there aren't obvious features to suggest that someone or something shoudn't be killable, ask yourselves: would the story (or the gameplay flow) normally continue regardless as usual after the kill? If the answer is yes, then it's likely that this trope is not at play. If you notice examples that do not really fit, please take some minutes to move them in the correct pages.
